            margin: 0 auto;
        }
        
        .cheatsheet {
            background: white;
            border-radius: 20px;
            padding: 40px;
            margin-bottom: 30px;
            box-shadow: 0 20px 60px rgba(0,0,0,0.3);
        }
        
        h1 {
            color: #667eea;
            font-size: 42px;
            margin-bottom: 10px;
            text-align: center;
            font-weight: 800;
        }
        
        h2 {
            color: #764ba2;
            font-size: 32px;
            margin: 30px 0 20px 0;
            padding-bottom: 10px;
            border-bottom: 3px solid #667eea;
        }
        
        h3 {
            color: #667eea;
            font-size: 24px;
            margin: 25px 0 15px 0;
        }
        
        .subtitle {
            text-align: center;
            color: #666;
            font-size: 18px;
            margin-bottom: 30px;
        }
        
        table {
            width: 100%;
            border-collapse: collapse;
            margin: 20px 0;
            font-size: 16px;
            box-shadow: 0 5px 15px rgba(0,0,0,0.1);
        }
        
        th {
            background: linear-gradient(135deg, #667eea 0%, #764ba2 100%);
            color: white;
            padding: 15px;
            text-align: left;
            font-weight: 600;
            font-size: 18px;
        }
        
        td {
            padding: 12px 15px;
            border-bottom: 1px solid #ddd;
        }
        
        tr:hover {
            background-color: #f5f5f5;
        }
        
        .best {
            background-color: #d4edda;
            font-weight: 600;
        }
        
        .good {
            background-color: #d1ecf1;
        }
        
        .ok {
            background-color: #fff3cd;
        }
        
        .bad {
            background-color: #f8d7da;
        }
        
        .terrible {
            background-color: #f5c6cb;
            font-weight: 600;
        }
        
        .notation-badge {
            display: inline-block;
            padding: 8px 16px;
            border-radius: 20px;
            font-weight: 600;
            font-size: 18px;
            margin: 5px;
        }
        
        .big-o {
            background-color: #667eea;
            color: white;
        }
        
        .big-theta {
            background-color: #f093fb;
            color: white;
        }
        
        .big-omega {
            background-color: #4facfe;
            color: white;
        }
        
        .code-example {
            background-color: #f8f9fa;
            border-left: 4px solid #667eea;
            padding: 15px;
            margin: 15px 0;
            font-family: 'Courier New', monospace;
            font-size: 14px;
            border-radius: 5px;
        }
        
        .visual-scale {
            display: flex;
            margin: 20px 0;
            gap: 10px;
        }
        
        .scale-item {
            flex: 1;
            padding: 20px;
            border-radius: 10px;
            text-align: center;
            color: white;
            font-weight: 600;
        }
        
        .emoji {
            font-size: 32px;
            display: block;
            margin-bottom: 10px;
        }
        
        .grid-2 {
            display: grid;
            grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr;
            gap: 20px;
            margin: 20px 0;
        }
        
        .card {
            background: #f8f9fa;
            padding: 20px;
            border-radius: 10px;
            border-left: 5px solid #667eea;
        }
        
        .highlight {
            background-color: #fff3cd;
            padding: 3px 8px;
            border-radius: 4px;
            font-weight: 600;
        }
    </style>
</head>
<body>
    <div class="container">
        
        <!-- CHEAT SHEET 1: NOTATION OVERVIEW -->
        <div class="cheatsheet">
            <h1>🎯 DSA COMPLEXITY CHEAT SHEET</h1>
            <p class="subtitle">Complete Guide to Time & Space Complexity</p>
            
            <h2>📊 The Three Notations</h2>
            
            <table>
                <tr>
                    <th>Notation</th>
                    <th>Symbol</th>
                    <th>Meaning</th>
                    <th>When to Use</th>
                    <th>Example</th>
                </tr>
                <tr>
                    <td><span class="notation-badge big-o">Big-O</span></td>
                    <td style="font-size: 24px; font-weight: bold;">O</td>
                    <td><strong>Upper Bound</strong><br>Worst case scenario</td>
                    <td>95% of the time<br>Interviews, Real world</td>
                    <td>Linear search: O(n)</td>
                </tr>
                <tr>
                    <td><span class="notation-badge big-theta">Big-Θ</span></td>
                    <td style="font-size: 24px; font-weight: bold;">Θ</td>
                    <td><strong>Tight Bound</strong><br>Exact/Average case</td>
                    <td>When best = worst<br>Academic analysis</td>
                    <td>Print all: Θ(n)</td>
                </tr>
                <tr>
                    <td><span class="notation-badge big-omega">Big-Ω</span></td>
                    <td style="font-size: 24px; font-weight: bold;">Ω</td>
                    <td><strong>Lower Bound</strong><br>Best case scenario</td>
                    <td>Rarely used<br>Theoretical CS</td>
                    <td>Linear search: Ω(1)</td>
                </tr>
            </table>
            
            <h2>⚡ Time Complexity Rankings</h2>
            
            <div class="visual-scale">
                <div class="scale-item best" style="background: linear-gradient(135deg, #11998e 0%, #38ef7d 100%);">
                    <span class="emoji">⚡⚡⚡</span>
                    <div>O(1)</div>
                    <div style="font-size: 12px;">CONSTANT</div>
                </div>
                <div class="scale-item best" style="background: linear-gradient(135deg, #2193b0 0%, #6dd5ed 100%);">
                    <span class="emoji">⚡⚡</span>
                    <div>O(log n)</div>
                    <div style="font-size: 12px;">LOGARITHMIC</div>
                </div>
                <div class="scale-item good" style="background: linear-gradient(135deg, #3a7bd5 0%, #3a6073 100%);">
                    <span class="emoji">✅</span>
                    <div>O(n)</div>
                    <div style="font-size: 12px;">LINEAR</div>
                </div>
                <div class="scale-item ok" style="background: linear-gradient(135deg, #f7971e 0%, #ffd200 100%);">
                    <span class="emoji">⚠️</span>
                    <div>O(n log n)</div>
                    <div style="font-size: 12px;">LINEARITHMIC</div>
                </div>
                <div class="scale-item bad" style="background: linear-gradient(135deg, #ee0979 0%, #ff6a00 100%);">
                    <span class="emoji">❌</span>
                    <div>O(n²)</div>
                    <div style="font-size: 12px;">QUADRATIC</div>
                </div>
                <div class="scale-item terrible" style="background: linear-gradient(135deg, #870000 0%, #190a05 100%);">
                    <span class="emoji">💀</span>
                    <div>O(2ⁿ)</div>
                    <div style="font-size: 12px;">EXPONENTIAL</div>
                </div>
            </div>
            
            <table>
                <tr>
                    <th>Complexity</th>
                    <th>Name</th>
                    <th>n=10</th>
                    <th>n=100</th>
                    <th>n=1000</th>
                    <th>Speed</th>
                </tr>
                <tr class="best">
                    <td><strong>O(1)</strong></td>
                    <td>Constant</td>
                    <td>1</td>
                    <td>1</td>
                    <td>1</td>
                    <td>⚡⚡⚡ BEST</td>
                </tr>
                <tr class="best">
                    <td><strong>O(log n)</strong></td>
                    <td>Logarithmic</td>
                    <td>3</td>
                    <td>7</td>
                    <td>10</td>
                    <td>⚡⚡ EXCELLENT</td>
                </tr>
                <tr class="good">
                    <td><strong>O(n)</strong></td>
                    <td>Linear</td>
                    <td>10</td>
                    <td>100</td>
                    <td>1,000</td>
                    <td>✅ GOOD</td>
                </tr>
                <tr class="ok">
                    <td><strong>O(n log n)</strong></td>
                    <td>Linearithmic</td>
                    <td>30</td>
                    <td>664</td>
                    <td>10,000</td>
                    <td>✅ DECENT</td>
                </tr>
                <tr class="bad">
                    <td><strong>O(n²)</strong></td>
                    <td>Quadratic</td>
                    <td>100</td>
                    <td>10,000</td>
                    <td>1,000,000</td>
                    <td>❌ BAD</td>
                </tr>
                <tr class="terrible">
                    <td><strong>O(2ⁿ)</strong></td>
                    <td>Exponential</td>
                    <td>1,024</td>
                    <td>∞</td>
                    <td>∞</td>
                    <td>💀 TERRIBLE</td>
                </tr>
                <tr class="terrible">
                    <td><strong>O(n!)</strong></td>
                    <td>Factorial</td>
                    <td>3,628,800</td>
                    <td>∞</td>
                    <td>∞</td>
                    <td>💀 WORST</td>
                </tr>
            </table>
            
            <h2>🧠 How to Calculate Time Complexity</h2>
            
            <div class="grid-2">
                <div class="card">
                    <h3>Rule 1: No Loops</h3>
                    <div class="code-example">
function getFirst(arr) {<br>
&nbsp;&nbsp;return arr[0];<br>
}<br>
<span class="highlight">O(1)</span>
                    </div>
                </div>
                
                <div class="card">
                    <h3>Rule 2: One Loop</h3>
                    <div class="code-example">
function printAll(arr) {<br>
&nbsp;&nbsp;for (let i=0; i&lt;arr.length; i++)<br>
&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;console.log(arr[i]);<br>
}<br>
<span class="highlight">O(n)</span>
                    </div>
                </div>
                
                <div class="card">
                    <h3>Rule 3: Nested Loops</h3>
                    <div class="code-example">
function printPairs(arr) {<br>
&nbsp;&nbsp;for (let i=0; i&lt;arr.length; i++)<br>
&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;for (let j=0; j&lt;arr.length; j++)<br>
&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;console.log(arr[i], arr[j]);<br>
}<br>
<span class="highlight">O(n²)</span>
                    </div>
                </div>
                
                <div class="card">
                    <h3>Rule 4: Halving</h3>
                    <div class="code-example">
function binarySearch(arr, target) {<br>
&nbsp;&nbsp;// Divides array in half each time<br>
&nbsp;&nbsp;// ...<br>
}<br>
<span class="highlight">O(log n)</span>
                    </div>
                </div>
            </div>
            
            <h2>💾 Space Complexity</h2>
            
            <table>
                <tr>
                    <th>Complexity</th>
                    <th>Meaning</th>
                    <th>Example</th>
                </tr>
                <tr class="best">
                    <td><strong>O(1)</strong></td>
                    <td>Constant space<br>Uses same memory regardless of input</td>
                    <td>
                        <div class="code-example">
let sum = 0;<br>
for (let i=0; i&lt;arr.length; i++)<br>
&nbsp;&nbsp;sum += arr[i];<br>
// Only 'sum' and 'i' variables
                        </div>
                    </td>
                </tr>
                <tr class="ok">
                    <td><strong>O(n)</strong></td>
                    <td>Linear space<br>Memory grows with input size</td>
                    <td>
                        <div class="code-example">
const result = [];<br>
for (let i=0; i&lt;arr.length; i++)<br>
&nbsp;&nbsp;result.push(arr[i] * 2);<br>
// 'result' array has n items
                        </div>
                    </td>
                </tr>
            </table>
            
            <h2>📝 Quick Reference Rules</h2>
            
            <table>
                <tr>
                    <th style="width: 40%;">Pattern</th>
                    <th style="width: 20%;">Time</th>
                    <th style="width: 20%;">Space</th>
                    <th style="width: 20%;">Example</th>
                </tr>
                <tr>
                    <td>Single loop through array</td>
                    <td>O(n)</td>
                    <td>O(1)</td>
                    <td>Sum array</td>
                </tr>
                <tr>
                    <td>Nested loops (same size)</td>
                    <td>O(n²)</td>
                    <td>O(1)</td>
                    <td>Find all pairs</td>
                </tr>
                <tr>
                    <td>Halving each iteration</td>
                    <td>O(log n)</td>
                    <td>O(1)</td>
                    <td>Binary search</td>
                </tr>
                <tr>
                    <td>Create new array of size n</td>
                    <td>O(n)</td>
                    <td>O(n)</td>
                    <td>Double all elements</td>
                </tr>
                <tr>
                    <td>Recursion (depth n)</td>
                    <td>O(n)</td>
                    <td>O(n)</td>
                    <td>Factorial</td>
                </tr>
                <tr>
                    <td>Divide & conquer</td>
                    <td>O(n log n)</td>
                    <td>O(n)</td>
                    <td>Merge sort</td>
                </tr>
            </table>
        </div>
        
        <!-- CHEAT SHEET 2: LANGUAGE-SPECIFIC -->
        <div class="cheatsheet">
            <h1>💻 COMPLEXITY IN JS, PYTHON, C</h1>
            <p class="subtitle">Language-Specific Time Complexity</p>
            
            <h2>🟨 JavaScript Operations</h2>
            
            <table>
                <tr>
                    <th>Operation</th>
                    <th>Code</th>
                    <th>Time</th>
                    <th>Notes</th>
                </tr>
                <tr class="best">
                    <td>Array access</td>
                    <td><code>arr[i]</code></td>
                    <td>O(1)</td>
                    <td>Direct index access</td>
                </tr>
                <tr class="best">
                    <td>Array push</td>
                    <td><code>arr.push(x)</code></td>
                    <td>O(1)</td>
                    <td>Add to end</td>
                </tr>
                <tr class="bad">
                    <td>Array unshift</td>
                    <td><code>arr.unshift(x)</code></td>
                    <td>O(n)</td>
                    <td>Add to start, shifts all</td>
                </tr>
                <tr class="best">
                    <td>Array pop</td>
                    <td><code>arr.pop()</code></td>
                    <td>O(1)</td>
                    <td>Remove from end</td>
                </tr>
                <tr class="bad">
                    <td>Array shift</td>
                    <td><code>arr.shift()</code></td>
                    <td>O(n)</td>
                    <td>Remove from start, shifts all</td>
                </tr>
                <tr class="bad">
                    <td>Array splice</td>
                    <td><code>arr.splice(i, 1)</code></td>
                    <td>O(n)</td>
                    <td>Remove/insert, shifts elements</td>
                </tr>
                <tr class="best">
                    <td>Array slice</td>
                    <td><code>arr.slice(start, end)</code></td>
                    <td>O(k)</td>
                    <td>k = size of slice</td>
                </tr>
                <tr class="good">
                    <td>Array indexOf</td>
                    <td><code>arr.indexOf(x)</code></td>
                    <td>O(n)</td>
                    <td>Linear search</td>
                </tr>
                <tr class="best">
                    <td>Map/Object get</td>
                    <td><code>map.get(key)</code></td>
                    <td>O(1)</td>
                    <td>Hash table lookup</td>
                </tr>
                <tr class="best">
                    <td>Map/Object set</td>
                    <td><code>map.set(k, v)</code></td>
                    <td>O(1)</td>
                    <td>Hash table insert</td>
                </tr>
                <tr class="best">
                    <td>Set add/has</td>
                    <td><code>set.add(x)</code></td>
                    <td>O(1)</td>
                    <td>Hash set operations</td>
                </tr>
                <tr class="ok">
                    <td>Array sort</td>
                    <td><code>arr.sort()</code></td>
                    <td>O(n log n)</td>
                    <td>Uses Timsort typically</td>
                </tr>
            </table>
            
            <h2>🐍 Python Operations</h2>
            
            <table>
                <tr>
                    <th>Operation</th>
                    <th>Code</th>
                    <th>Time</th>
                    <th>Notes</th>
                </tr>
                <tr class="best">
                    <td>List access</td>
                    <td><code>arr[i]</code></td>
                    <td>O(1)</td>
                    <td>Direct index</td>
                </tr>
                <tr class="best">
                    <td>List append</td>
                    <td><code>arr.append(x)</code></td>
                    <td>O(1)</td>
                    <td>Amortized constant</td>
                </tr>
                <tr class="bad">
                    <td>List insert</td>
                    <td><code>arr.insert(0, x)</code></td>
                    <td>O(n)</td>
                    <td>Shifts elements</td>
                </tr>
                <tr class="best">
                    <td>List pop (end)</td>
                    <td><code>arr.pop()</code></td>
                    <td>O(1)</td>
                    <td>Remove last</td>
                </tr>
                <tr class="bad">
                    <td>List pop (start)</td>
                    <td><code>arr.pop(0)</code></td>
                    <td>O(n)</td>
                    <td>Shifts all elements</td>
                </tr>
                <tr class="good">
                    <td>List index</td>
                    <td><code>arr.index(x)</code></td>
                    <td>O(n)</td>
                    <td>Linear search</td>
                </tr>
                <tr class="best">
                    <td>Dict get/set</td>
                    <td><code>dict[key]</code></td>
                    <td>O(1)</td>
                    <td>Hash table</td>
                </tr>
                <tr class="best">
                    <td>Set add/in</td>
                    <td><code>set.add(x)</code></td>
                    <td>O(1)</td>
                    <td>Hash set</td>
                </tr>
                <tr class="ok">
                    <td>List sort</td>
                    <td><code>arr.sort()</code></td>
                    <td>O(n log n)</td>
                    <td>Timsort algorithm</td>
                </tr>
                <tr class="ok">
                    <td>Sorted</td>
                    <td><code>sorted(arr)</code></td>
                    <td>O(n log n)</td>
                    <td>Returns new sorted list</td>
                </tr>
            </table>
            
            <h2>⚙️ C Operations</h2>
            
            <table>
                <tr>
                    <th>Operation</th>
                    <th>Code</th>
                    <th>Time</th>
                    <th>Notes</th>
                </tr>
                <tr class="best">
                    <td>Array access</td>
                    <td><code>arr[i]</code></td>
                    <td>O(1)</td>
                    <td>Pointer arithmetic</td>
                </tr>
                <tr class="best">
                    <td>Pointer deref</td>
                    <td><code>*ptr</code></td>
                    <td>O(1)</td>
                    <td>Direct memory access</td>
                </tr>
                <tr class="good">
                    <td>String length</td>
                    <td><code>strlen(str)</code></td>
                    <td>O(n)</td>
                    <td>Scans until '\0'</td>
                </tr>
                <tr class="good">
                    <td>String copy</td>
                    <td><code>strcpy(dest, src)</code></td>
                    <td>O(n)</td>
                    <td>Copies each char</td>
                </tr>
                <tr class="good">
                    <td>String compare</td>
                    <td><code>strcmp(s1, s2)</code></td>
                    <td>O(n)</td>
                    <td>Compares each char</td>
                </tr>
                <tr class="best">
                    <td>Memory alloc</td>
                    <td><code>malloc(size)</code></td>
                    <td>O(1)</td>
                    <td>Typically constant</td>
                </tr>
                <tr class="ok">
                    <td>qsort</td>
                    <td><code>qsort(arr, n, ...)</code></td>
                    <td>O(n log n)</td>
                    <td>Quicksort average</td>
                </tr>
            </table>
            
            <h2>🔄 Converting Between Languages</h2>
            
            <table>
                <tr>
                    <th>Operation</th>
                    <th>JavaScript</th>
                    <th>Python</th>
                    <th>C</th>
                    <th>Complexity</th>
                </tr>
                <tr>
                    <td>Create array</td>
                    <td><code>const arr = [1,2,3]</code></td>
                    <td><code>arr = [1,2,3]</code></td>
                    <td><code>int arr[] = {1,2,3}</code></td>
                    <td>O(n)</td>
                </tr>
                <tr>
                    <td>Get length</td>
                    <td><code>arr.length</code></td>
                    <td><code>len(arr)</code></td>
                    <td><code>sizeof(arr)/sizeof(arr[0])</code></td>
                    <td>O(1)</td>
                </tr>
                <tr>
                    <td>Loop array</td>
                    <td><code>for (let x of arr)</code></td>
                    <td><code>for x in arr:</code></td>
                    <td><code>for (i=0; i&lt;n; i++)</code></td>
                    <td>O(n)</td>
                </tr>
                <tr>
                    <td>Find max</td>
                    <td><code>Math.max(...arr)</code></td>
                    <td><code>max(arr)</code></td>
                    <td>Manual loop</td>
                    <td>O(n)</td>
                </tr>
                <tr>
                    <td>Sort array</td>
                    <td><code>arr.sort((a,b)=>a-b)</code></td>
                    <td><code>arr.sort()</code></td>
                    <td><code>qsort(...)</code></td>
                    <td>O(n log n)</td>
                </tr>
                <tr>
                    <td>Reverse</td>
                    <td><code>arr.reverse()</code></td>
                    <td><code>arr.reverse()</code></td>
                    <td>Manual swap</td>
                    <td>O(n)</td>
                </tr>
            </table>
        </div>
        
        <!-- CHEAT SHEET 3: COMMON ALGORITHMS -->
        <div class="cheatsheet">
            <h1>🚀 ALGORITHM COMPLEXITY REFERENCE</h1>
            <p class="subtitle">Common Algorithms & Their Complexities</p>
            
            <h2>🔍 Searching Algorithms</h2>
            
            <table>
                <tr>
                    <th>Algorithm</th>
                    <th>Best Case</th>
                    <th>Average Case</th>
                    <th>Worst Case</th>
                    <th>Space</th>
                    <th>Notes</th>
                </tr>
                <tr>
                    <td><strong>Linear Search</strong></td>
                    <td class="best">Ω(1)</td>
                    <td class="good">Θ(n)</td>
                    <td class="good">O(n)</td>
                    <td>O(1)</td>
                    <td>Found first try / Check all</td>
                </tr>
                <tr>
                    <td><strong>Binary Search</strong></td>
                    <td class="best">Ω(1)</td>
                    <td class="best">Θ(log n)</td>
                    <td class="best">O(log n)</td>
                    <td>O(1)</td>
                    <td>Requires sorted array</td>
                </tr>
            </table>
            
            <h2>📊 Sorting Algorithms</h2>
            
            <table>
                <tr>
                    <th>Algorithm</th>
                    <th>Best</th>
                    <th>Average</th>
                    <th>Worst</th>
                    <th>Space</th>
                    <th>Stable?</th>
                </tr>
                <tr>
                    <td><strong>Bubble Sort</strong></td>
                    <td class="good">Ω(n)</td>
                    <td class="bad">Θ(n²)</td>
                    <td class="bad">O(n²)</td>
                    <td>O(1)</td>
                    <td>✅ Yes</td>
                </tr>
                <tr>
                    <td><strong>Selection Sort</strong></td>
                    <td class="bad">Ω(n²)</td>
                    <td class="bad">Θ(n²)</td>
                    <td class="bad">O(n²)</td>
                    <td>O(1)</td>
                    <td>❌ No</td>
                </tr>
                <tr>
                    <td><strong>Insertion Sort</strong></td>
                    <td class="good">Ω(n)</td>
                    <td class="bad">Θ(n²)</td>
                    <td class="bad">O(n²)</td>
                    <td>O(1)</td>
                    <td>✅ Yes</td>
                </tr>
                <tr>
                    <td><strong>Merge Sort</strong></td>
                    <td class="ok">Ω(n log n)</td>
                    <td class="ok">Θ(n log n)</td>
                    <td class="ok">O(n log n)</td>
                    <td>O(n)</td>
                    <td>✅ Yes</td>
                </tr>
                <tr>
                    <td><strong>Quick Sort</strong></td>
                    <td class="ok">Ω(n log n)</td>
                    <td class="ok">Θ(n log n)</td>
                    <td class="bad">O(n²)</td>
                    <td>O(log n)</td>
                    <td>❌ No</td>
                </tr>
                <tr>
                    <td><strong>Heap Sort</strong></td>
                    <td class="ok">Ω(n log n)</td>
                    <td class="ok">Θ(n log n)</td>
                    <td class="ok">O(n log n)</td>
                    <td>O(1)</td>
                    <td>❌ No</td>
                </tr>
            </table>
            
            <h2>🌳 Data Structure Operations</h2>
            
            <table>
                <tr>
                    <th>Data Structure</th>
                    <th>Access</th>
                    <th>Search</th>
                    <th>Insert</th>
                    <th>Delete</th>
                    <th>Space</th>
                </tr>
                <tr>
                    <td><strong>Array</strong></td>
                    <td class="best">O(1)</td>
                    <td class="good">O(n)</td>
                    <td class="good">O(n)</td>
                    <td class="good">O(n)</td>
                    <td>O(n)</td>
                </tr>
                <tr>
                    <td><strong>Stack</strong></td>
                    <td class="good">O(n)</td>
                    <td class="good">O(n)</td>
                    <td class="best">O(1)</td>
                    <td class="best">O(1)</td>
                    <td>O(n)</td>
                </tr>
                <tr>
                    <td><strong>Queue</strong></td>
                    <td class="good">O(n)</td>
                    <td class="good">O(n)</td>
                    <td class="best">O(1)</td>
                    <td class="best">O(1)</td>
                    <td>O(n)</td>
                </tr>
                <tr>
                    <td><strong>Linked List</strong></td>
                    <td class="good">O(n)</td>
                    <td class="good">O(n)</td>
                    <td class="best">O(1)</td>
                    <td class="best">O(1)</td>
                    <td>O(n)</td>
                </tr>
                <tr>
                    <td><strong>Hash Table</strong></td>
                    <td>N/A</td>
                    <td class="best">O(1)</td>
                    <td class="best">O(1)</td>
                    <td class="best">O(1)</td>
                    <td>O(n)</td>
                </tr>
                <tr>
                    <td><strong>Binary Search Tree</strong></td>
                    <td class="best">O(log n)</td>
                    <td class="best">O(log n)</td>
                    <td class="best">O(log n)</td>
                    <td class="best">O(log n)</td>
                    <td>O(n)</td>
                </tr>
                <tr>
                    <td><strong>Heap</strong></td>
                    <td class="good">O(n)</td>
                    <td class="good">O(n)</td>
                    <td class="best">O(log n)</td>
                    <td class="best">O(log n)</td>
                    <td>O(n)</td>
                </tr>
            </table>
            
            <h2>💡 Quick Decision Guide</h2>
            
            <div class="grid-2">
                <div class="card">
                    <h3>✅ Use O(1) - Constant</h3>
                    <ul>
                        <li>Array index access</li>
                        <li>Hash map lookup</li>
                        <li>Stack push/pop</li>
                        <li>Simple calculations</li>
                    </ul>
                </div>
                
                <div class="card">
                    <h3>✅ Use O(log n) - Logarithmic</h3>
                    <ul>
                        <li>Binary search in sorted array</li>
                        <li>Balanced tree operations</li>
                        <li>Divide and conquer</li>
                    </ul>
                </div>
                
                <div class="card">
                    <h3>✅ Use O(n) - Linear</h3>
                    <ul>
                        <li>Single loop through data</li>
                        <li>Linear search</li>
                        <li>Array traversal</li>
                        <li>Most array operations</li>
                    </ul>
                </div>
                
                <div class="card">
                    <h3>✅ Use O(n log n) - Linearithmic</h3>
                    <ul>
                        <li>Efficient sorting (Merge, Quick)</li>
                        <li>Divide and conquer with merge</li>
                        <li>Best we can do for sorting</li>
                    </ul>
                </div>
                
                <div class="card">
                    <h3>❌ Avoid O(n²) - Quadratic</h3>
                    <ul>
                        <li>Nested loops</li>
                        <li>Bubble/Selection sort</li>
                        <li>Comparing all pairs</li>
                        <li>Only for small data (&lt;100 items)</li>
                    </ul>
                </div>
                
                <div class="card">
                    <h3>💀 Never Use O(2ⁿ) - Exponential</h3>
                    <ul>
                        <li>Naive recursive Fibonacci</li>
                        <li>Brute force solutions</li>
                        <li>Trying all subsets</li>
                        <li>Computer will freeze!</li>
                    </ul>
                </div>
            </div>
            
            <h2>🎓 Interview Tips</h2>
            
            <div style="background: #fff3cd; border-left: 5px solid #ffc107; padding: 20px; border-radius: 10px; margin: 20px 0;">
                <h3 style="margin-top: 0; color: #856404;">When Interviewer Asks "What's the time complexity?"</h3>
                <ul style="margin: 10px 0; padding-left: 20px;">
                    <li><strong>They mean:</strong> Big-O (worst case)</li>
                    <li><strong>Say:</strong> "O(n)" not "Θ(n)" or "Ω(n)"</li>
                    <li><strong>Explain:</strong> "In the worst case, we check all n elements, so O(n)"</li>
                    <li><strong>Bonus:</strong> Mention space complexity too: "O(n) time, O(1) space"</li>
                </ul>
            </div>
            
            <div style="text-align: center; margin-top: 40px; padding: 30px; background: linear-gradient(135deg, #667eea 0%, #764ba2 100%); color: white; border-radius: 15px;">
                <h2 style="color: white; margin-bottom: 15px;">🎯 Remember</h2>
                <p style="font-size: 18px; margin: 10px 0;"><strong>Consistency > Intensity</strong></p>
                <p style="font-size: 18px; margin: 10px 0;"><strong>Understand > Memorize</strong></p>
                <p style="font-size: 18px; margin: 10px 0;"><strong>Practice > Theory</strong></p>
                <p style="font-size: 24px; margin-top: 20px;">You got this! 🔥💪</p>
            </div>
        </div>
        
    </div>
</body>
</html>

# **HOW TO USE THIS CHEAT SHEET:**

## **STEP 1: OPEN THE FILE**

1. Download the `complexity-cheatsheet.html` file
2. Open it in any web browser (Chrome, Firefox, Edge, Safari)
3. You'll see 3 beautiful, colorful cheat sheets!

---

## **STEP 2: TAKE SCREENSHOTS**

### **Method 1: Full Page Screenshot (Best)**

**Chrome:**
1. Press `F12` (open DevTools)
2. Press `Ctrl+Shift+P` (Command Palette)
3. Type "screenshot"
4. Choose "Capture full size screenshot"
5. Saves entire page as PNG!

**Firefox:**
1. Press `F12`
2. Click `...` menu
3. Click "Take a screenshot"
4. Choose "Save full page"

---

### **Method 2: Manual Screenshots**

1. Zoom out browser (Ctrl + `-` or Cmd + `-`)
2. Take screenshot of each section
3. Use Windows Snipping Tool or Mac Screenshot (Cmd+Shift+4)

---

## **STEP 3: SAVE & USE**

Save the images to:
- 📱 Your phone (for quick reference)
- 💻 Desktop wallpaper
- 📁 Notion page (paste images)
- 🖨️ Print and stick on wall!

---

## **WHAT'S IN THE CHEAT SHEET:**

### **📊 Sheet 1: Notation Overview**
- Big-O, Big-Θ, Big-Ω explained
- Time complexity rankings
- Visual scale (color-coded)
- Operations count comparison
- How to calculate rules
- Space complexity

### **💻 Sheet 2: Language-Specific**
- JavaScript operations & complexity
- Python operations & complexity  
- C operations & complexity
- Converting between languages

### **🚀 Sheet 3: Algorithms Reference**
- Searching algorithms
- Sorting algorithms
- Data structure operations
- Decision guide
- Interview tips

---

## **COLORS MEANING:**

🟢 **Green** = Best (O(1), O(log n))
🔵 **Blue** = Good (O(n))
🟡 **Yellow** = Okay (O(n log n))
🔴 **Red** = Bad (O(n²))
⚫ **Black** = Terrible (O(2ⁿ), O(n!))

1. Go to https://github.com
2. Sign up (free)

1. Click **"New repository"**

3. Make it **Public**
4. Check **"Add a README file"**
5. Click **"Create repository"**

### **Step 3: Upload Your File**

2. Drag `complexity-cheatsheet.html`
3. Rename it to **`index.html`** (important!)
4. Click **"Commit changes"**

### **Step 4: Enable GitHub Pages**
1. Go to **Settings** tab
2. Scroll to **"Pages"** in left sidebar
3. Under **"Source"**, select **"main"** branch
4. Click **"Save"**
5. Wait 1-2 minutes

### **Step 5: Get Your URL**
Your site will be live at:
```

```

---

# **LET'S START: PYTHON SYNTAX CRASH COURSE**

## **You already know JavaScript, so I'll teach Python by COMPARISON!**

---

## **PART 1: VARIABLES & DATA TYPES (10 minutes)**

### **JavaScript vs Python:**

**JavaScript:**
```javascript
let name = "Faizan";
const age = 25;
let isStudent = true;
```

**Python:**
```python
name = "Faizan"
age = 25
is_student = True  # Note: Capital T
```

**Key differences:**
- ❌ No `let`, `const`, `var` in Python
- ✅ Just assign: `variable = value`
- ⚠️ Boolean: `True`/`False` (capital first letter)
- ⚠️ `None` instead of `null`

---

## **PART 2: LISTS (ARRAYS) (15 minutes)**

**JavaScript:**
```javascript
const arr = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5];

arr.push(6);        // Add to end
arr.pop();          // Remove from end
arr.unshift(0);     // Add to start
arr.shift();        // Remove from start
arr[0];             // Access
arr.length;         // Length
```

**Python:**
```python
arr = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]

arr.append(6)       # Add to end
arr.pop()           # Remove from end
arr.insert(0, 0)    # Add to start
arr.pop(0)          # Remove from start
arr[0]              # Access
len(arr)            # Length
```

**Key differences:**
- ✅ `append()` instead of `push()`
- ✅ `len(arr)` instead of `arr.length`
- ✅ No semicolons!

---

### **SLICING (Python superpower!):**

**JavaScript:**
```javascript
const arr = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5];
const sub = arr.slice(1, 4);  // [2, 3, 4]
```

**Python:**
```python
arr = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]
sub = arr[1:4]  # [2, 3, 4]  ← EASIER!
```

**Python slicing:**
```python
arr[start:end]       # From start to end-1
arr[:3]              # First 3 items
arr[2:]              # From index 2 to end
arr[-1]              # Last item
arr[-3:]             # Last 3 items
arr[::-1]            # Reverse! (Magic!)
```

---

## **PART 3: LOOPS (10 minutes)**

**JavaScript:**
```javascript
// For loop
for (let i = 0; i < 10; i++) {
    console.log(i);
}

// For...of loop
for (let num of arr) {
    console.log(num);
}

// While loop
while (i < 10) {
    console.log(i);
    i++;
}
```

**Python:**
```python
# For loop with range
for i in range(10):
    print(i)

# For loop over list
for num in arr:
    print(num)

# While loop
while i < 10:
    print(i)
    i += 1
```

**Key differences:**
- ✅ `range(10)` creates 0-9
- ✅ `for item in list:` (cleaner!)
- ⚠️ **Indentation matters!** (No `{}` braces)

---

## **PART 4: FUNCTIONS (10 minutes)**

**JavaScript:**
```javascript
function add(a, b) {
    return a + b;
}

const result = add(5, 3);
```

**Python:**
```python
def add(a, b):
    return a + b

result = add(5, 3)
```

**Key differences:**
- ✅ `def` instead of `function`
- ✅ Colon `:` after parameters
- ✅ **Indentation** defines function body (no `{}`)

---

## **PART 5: DICTIONARIES (OBJECTS) (10 minutes)**

**JavaScript:**
```javascript
const person = {
    name: "Faizan",
    age: 25,
    city: "Mumbai"
};

person.name;        // Access
person["age"];      // Access
person.job = "Dev"; // Add
```

**Python:**
```python
person = {
    "name": "Faizan",
    "age": 25,
    "city": "Mumbai"
}

person["name"]      # Access (brackets!)
person["job"] = "Dev"  # Add
```

**Key differences:**
- ⚠️ Use `person["key"]` not `person.key`
- ✅ Keys must be strings (usually)

---

## **PART 6: CONDITIONALS (5 minutes)**

**JavaScript:**
```javascript
if (x > 10) {
    console.log("Big");
} else if (x > 5) {
    console.log("Medium");
} else {
    console.log("Small");
}
```

**Python:**
```python
if x > 10:
    print("Big")
elif x > 5:        # elif, not else if!
    print("Medium")
else:
    print("Small")
```

**Key differences:**
- ✅ `elif` instead of `else if`
- ✅ Colon `:` after condition
- ✅ Indentation (no braces)

---

## **PART 7: COMMON PATTERNS (10 minutes)**

### **Print vs Console.log:**

```javascript
console.log("Hello");
console.log("Value:", value);
```

```python
print("Hello")
print("Value:", value)
```

---

### **String methods:**

```javascript
str.toUpperCase()
str.toLowerCase()
str.includes("sub")
str.split(" ")
```

```python
str.upper()
str.lower()
"sub" in str
str.split(" ")
```

---

### **Math:**

```javascript
Math.max(1, 2, 3)
Math.min(1, 2, 3)
Math.floor(3.7)
```

```python
max([1, 2, 3])  # Need list!
```

# **WEEK 2: CORE PATTERNS**

- Interviews test pattern recognition

# **THE 5 MUST-KNOW PATTERNS:**

1. **Two Pointers** ⭐⭐⭐ (Very common)
2. **Sliding Window** ⭐⭐⭐ (Very common)
3. **Frequency Counter / Hash Map** ⭐⭐⭐ (Very common)
4. **Fast & Slow Pointers** ⭐⭐ (Common)
5. **In-Place Modification** ⭐⭐ (Common)

# **LET'S START: PATTERN 1 - TWO POINTERS** 🎯

---

## **WHAT IS TWO POINTERS?**

**Simple idea:**
Use TWO variables to track positions in array/string.

**When to use:**
- Array or string problems
- Finding pairs
- Comparing elements
- Sorted arrays
- Palindrome checking

---

## **THE TEMPLATE:**

```javascript
function twoPointerPattern(arr) {
    let left = 0;                    // Start pointer
    let right = arr.length - 1;       // End pointer
    
    while (left < right) {
        // Do something with arr[left] and arr[right]
        
        // Move pointers based on condition
        if (someCondition) {
            left++;   // Move left forward
        } else {
            right--;  // Move right backward
        }
    }
}
```

**Time:** O(n) - One pass through array
**Space:** O(1) - Only two variables

---

## **EXAMPLE 1: CHECK IF PALINDROME**

**Problem:** Check if string reads same forwards and backwards

**Examples:**
- "racecar" → true
- "hello" → false
- "madam" → true

---

### **SOLUTION:**

```javascript
function isPalindrome(str) {
    let left = 0;
    let right = str.length - 1;
    
    while (left < right) {
        if (str[left] !== str[right]) {
            return false;  // Not matching, not palindrome
        }
        left++;
        right--;
    }
    
    return true;  // All matched, is palindrome
}

// Test
console.log(isPalindrome("racecar"));  // true
console.log(isPalindrome("hello"));    // false
console.log(isPalindrome("madam"));    // true
```

---

### **HOW IT WORKS:**

```
"racecar"
 ^     ^   left=0, right=6, r===r ✅, move both
  ^   ^    left=1, right=5, a===a ✅, move both
   ^ ^     left=2, right=4, c===c ✅, move both
    ^      left=3, right=3, left >= right, STOP
Result: true
```

---

## **EXAMPLE 2: TWO SUM (SORTED ARRAY)**

**Problem:** Find two numbers that add up to target

**Input:** `arr = [1, 2, 3, 4, 6]`, `target = 6`
**Output:** `[1, 3]` (because arr[1]=2 and arr[3]=4, and 2+4=6)

---

### **SOLUTION:**

```javascript
function twoSum(arr, target) {
    let left = 0;
    let right = arr.length - 1;
    
    while (left < right) {
        const sum = arr[left] + arr[right];
        
        if (sum === target) {
            return [left, right];  // Found!
        } else if (sum < target) {
            left++;   // Need bigger sum, move left
        } else {
            right--;  // Need smaller sum, move right
        }
    }
    
    return [];  // Not found
}

// Test
console.log(twoSum([1, 2, 3, 4, 6], 6));  // [1, 3]
console.log(twoSum([2, 7, 11, 15], 9));   // [0, 1]
```

---

### **HOW IT WORKS:**

```
arr = [1, 2, 3, 4, 6], target = 6

Step 1: left=0, right=4
        arr[0]=1, arr[4]=6
        sum = 1+6 = 7
        7 > 6, so right--

Step 2: left=0, right=3
        arr[0]=1, arr[3]=4
        sum = 1+4 = 5
        5 < 6, so left++

Step 3: left=1, right=3
        arr[1]=2, arr[3]=4
        sum = 2+4 = 6
        6 === 6, FOUND! Return [1, 3]
```

---

## **EXAMPLE 3: REVERSE STRING**

**Problem:** Reverse string in-place

**Input:** `"hello"`
**Output:** `"olleh"`

---

### **SOLUTION:**

```javascript
function reverseString(s) {
    const arr = s.split('');  // Convert to array
    let left = 0;
    let right = arr.length - 1;
    
    while (left < right) {
        // Swap
        [arr[left], arr[right]] = [arr[right], arr[left]];
        left++;
        right--;
    }
    
    return arr.join('');  // Convert back to string
}

// Test
console.log(reverseString("hello"));   // "olleh"
console.log(reverseString("world"));   // "dlrow"
```

---


- Use two pointers
- One for reading, one for writing
- If different, copy to write position

```javascript
}

console.log(removeDuplicates([1, 1, 2, 2, 3, 4, 4]));  // 4
```

### **PROBLEM 3: Move Zeros to End**

**Example:**

```javascript
Input: [0, 1, 0, 3, 12]
Output: [1, 3, 12, 0, 0]
```

- Use two pointers
- One pointer for non-zero position
- One pointer scanning array

**YOUR CODE:**
```javascript

    // YOUR CODE HERE
}

const arr = [0, 1, 0, 3, 12];
moveZeros(arr);
console.log(arr);  // [1, 3, 12, 0, 0]
```

### **PROBLEM 4: Container With Most Water**

**Description:** Find two lines that form container with most water.

**Example:**
```javascript
Input: heights = [1, 8, 6, 2, 5, 4, 8, 3, 7]
Output: 49
```

**Hint:**
- Two pointers: start and end
- Area = min(height[left], height[right]) × (right - left)
- Move pointer with smaller height

**YOUR CODE:**
```javascript
function maxArea(heights) {
    // YOUR CODE HERE
}

console.log(maxArea([1, 8, 6, 2, 5, 4, 8, 3, 7]));  // 49
```

### **PROBLEM 5: Three Sum**

**Example:**
```javascript

Output: [[-1, -1, 2], [-1, 0, 1]]
```

**Hint:**
- Sort array first
- Fix one number, use two pointers for other two
- Skip duplicates

**YOUR CODE:**
```javascript

    // YOUR CODE HERE
}

console.log(threeSum([-1, 0, 1, 2, -1, -4]));
// [[-1, -1, 2], [-1, 0, 1]]
```

# **YOUR ANSWER ANALYSIS:**

## **WHAT YOU SAID:**

> "O(n) because we don't know how many elements are in the array"

**This part is CORRECT!** ✅

> "If it was 'find if target is available' then it can be O(1)"

**This part is WRONG!** ❌

---

# **THE CORRECTION:**

## **FINDING AN ELEMENT IS ALWAYS O(n) IN UNSORTED ARRAY!**

**Why?**

Because in the **WORST CASE**, you might have to check **ALL n elements!**

---

## **EXAMPLE:**

```javascript
function contains(arr, target) {
    for (let i = 0; i < arr.length; i++) {
        if (arr[i] === target) {
            return true;  // Found it!
        }
    }
    return false;  // Not found
}
```

**Best case:** Target is at index 0 → Find in 1 step → Ω(1) ✅
**Worst case:** Target is at end or not there → Check all n → **O(n)** ✅
**Average case:** Check n/2 elements → Still Θ(n) ✅

---

## **WHEN IS IT O(1)?**

**ONLY when you know the EXACT position!**

```javascript
function getFirst(arr) {
    return arr[0];  // O(1) - Direct access!
}

function getLast(arr) {
    return arr[arr.length - 1];  // O(1) - Direct access!
}
```

**Why O(1)?**
- You're not searching
- You're directly accessing a known position
- Doesn't matter if array has 10 or 1 million items

---

## **YOUR CONFUSION:**

You thought:
> "Find if target is available" = O(1)

**Reality:**
- **Finding/Searching** (don't know position) = **O(n)**
- **Accessing** (know exact position) = **O(1)**

---

# **THE KEY DIFFERENCE:**

| Operation | Complexity | Why |
|-----------|-----------|-----|
| **arr[5]** | O(1) | Direct access, know position |
| **arr[0]** | O(1) | Direct access, know position |
| **arr.length** | O(1) | Just a property |
| **Find 7 in array** | O(n) | Must search, worst case check all |
| **Check if exists** | O(n) | Must search, worst case check all |

---

# **EXCEPTION: HASH MAP LOOKUP = O(1)**

**This is O(1):**

```javascript
const map = new Map();
map.set('name', 'Faizan');
map.set('age', 25);

console.log(map.get('name'));  // O(1) ✅
console.log(map.has('age'));   // O(1) ✅
```

**Why?**
- Hash maps use hashing
- Direct access to value
- Don't need to search

**BUT regular array search is O(n)!**

---

# **YOUR UNDERSTANDING:**

**You got:**
✅ O(n) for the loop (correct!)
✅ Understanding that we don't know array size (correct!)

**You confused:**
❌ Thought "finding" could be O(1) in arrays (wrong!)

**Reality:**
- **Array searching** = O(n)
- **Array accessing** = O(1)
- **Hash map lookup** = O(1)

---

# **QUICK QUIZ TO SOLIDIFY:**

**What's the complexity?**

```javascript

function getMiddle(arr) {
    const mid = Math.floor(arr.length / 2);
    return arr[mid];
}
```

**Your answer:** __________

<details>
<summary>Answer</summary>
**O(1)** - Direct access to middle element
</details>

---

```javascript
// Problem 2
function findMax(arr) {
    let max = arr[0];
    for (let i = 1; i < arr.length; i++) {
        if (arr[i] > max) {
            max = arr[i];
        }
    }
    return max;
}
```

**Your answer:** __________

<details>
<summary>Answer</summary>
**O(n)** - Must check all n elements
</details>

---

```javascript
// Problem 3
function contains(arr, target) {
    for (let i = 0; i < arr.length; i++) {
        if (arr[i] === target) {
            return true;
        }
    }
    return false;
}
```

**Your answer:** __________

<details>
<summary>Answer</summary>
**O(n)** - Worst case: check all elements
</details>

---

```javascript
// Problem 4
const map = new Map();
map.set('key', 'value');
console.log(map.get('key'));
```

**Your answer:** __________

<details>
<summary>Answer</summary>
**O(1)** - Hash map lookup is constant time
</details>

---

# **SUMMARY:**

## **✅ YOU UNDERSTAND:**
- Loops = O(n)
- Don't know array size = still analyze complexity

## **❌ YOU MISUNDERSTOOD:**
- Searching in array ≠ O(1)
- Only direct access is O(1)

## **✅ NOW YOU KNOW:**
- **Search** (linear scan) = O(n)
- **Access** (direct index) = O(1)
- **Hash map** lookup = O(1)

# **SMALL CLARIFICATION:**

Your answer for **getMiddle** was slightly confusing in wording, but your **conclusion was right!**

You said:
> "it can also be like o(n) but..."

**No!** getMiddle is **NEVER O(n)**. It's **ALWAYS O(1)**.

**Why?**
```javascript
function getMiddle(arr) {
    const mid = Math.floor(arr.length / 2);  // O(1) - just math
    return arr[mid];                          // O(1) - direct access
}
```

- `arr.length` → O(1) (just a property)
- `Math.floor()` → O(1) (simple calculation)
- `arr[mid]` → O(1) (direct index access)

**Total: O(1) + O(1) + O(1) = O(1)** ✅

**It can NEVER be O(n)** because there's no loop!

---

# **GOLDEN RULES YOU NOW KNOW:**

## **O(1) - CONSTANT:**
- Direct array access: `arr[i]`
- Getting length: `arr.length`
- Simple math: `a + b`, `Math.floor(x)`
- Hash map operations: `map.get()`, `map.set()`
- No loops!

## **O(n) - LINEAR:**
- Single loop through array
- Searching for element
- Finding max/min
- Checking all elements

## **O(n²) - QUADRATIC:**
- Nested loops
- Comparing all pairs

# **TWO POINTERS - PRACTICE PROBLEMS**

After I teach you the concept, you'll solve these:

---

## **PROBLEM 1: Valid Palindrome** ⭐ (EASIEST)

**Description:** 
Check if a string is a palindrome, ignoring spaces, punctuation, and case.

**Examples:**
```javascript
Input: "A man, a plan, a canal: Panama"
Output: true

Input: "race a car"
Output: false

Input: "Was it a car or a cat I saw?"
Output: true
```

**Difficulty:** Easy
**Time to solve:** 15-20 minutes

---

## **PROBLEM 2: Remove Duplicates from Sorted Array** ⭐⭐

**Description:**
Given a sorted array, remove duplicates in-place and return the new length.

**Examples:**
```javascript
Input: [1, 1, 2, 2, 3, 4, 4]
Output: 4
// Array becomes: [1, 2, 3, 4, _, _, _]

Input: [0, 0, 1, 1, 1, 2, 2, 3, 3, 4]
Output: 5
// Array becomes: [0, 1, 2, 3, 4, _, _, _, _, _]
```

**Difficulty:** Easy
**Time to solve:** 15-20 minutes

---

## **PROBLEM 3: Move Zeros to End** ⭐⭐

**Description:**
Move all zeros to the end while maintaining the order of non-zero elements.

**Examples:**
```javascript
Input: [0, 1, 0, 3, 12]
Output: [1, 3, 12, 0, 0]

Input: [0, 0, 1]
Output: [1, 0, 0]

Input: [2, 1]
Output: [2, 1]
```

**Difficulty:** Easy-Medium
**Time to solve:** 20-25 minutes

---

## **PROBLEM 4: Container With Most Water** ⭐⭐⭐

**Description:**
Given heights of vertical lines, find two lines that together with x-axis form a container with the most water.

**Examples:**
```javascript
Input: [1, 8, 6, 2, 5, 4, 8, 3, 7]
Output: 49
// Explanation: Max area between heights[1]=8 and heights[8]=7
// Area = min(8, 7) × (8 - 1) = 7 × 7 = 49

Input: [1, 1]
Output: 1
```

**Difficulty:** Medium
**Time to solve:** 30-40 minutes

---

## **PROBLEM 5: Three Sum** ⭐⭐⭐⭐

**Description:**
Find all unique triplets in array that sum to zero.

**Examples:**
```javascript
Input: [-1, 0, 1, 2, -1, -4]
Output: [[-1, -1, 2], [-1, 0, 1]]

Input: [0, 0, 0]
Output: [[0, 0, 0]]

Input: [0, 1, 1]
Output: []
// No solution
```

**Difficulty:** Medium-Hard
**Time to solve:** 40-50 minutes

---

| Problem | Difficulty | Time | Important? |
|---------|-----------|------|------------|
| 1. Valid Palindrome | ⭐ Easy | 15-20 min | ✅ Must Know |

Consider the number of unique elements in `nums` to be `k​​​​​`​​​​​​. After removing duplicates, return the number of unique elements `k`.
The first `k` elements of `nums` should contain the unique numbers in sorted order. The remaining elements beyond index `k - 1` can be ignored.
Custom Judge:
The judge will test your solution with the following code:

```
int[] nums = [...]; // Input array
int[] expectedNums = [...]; // The expected answer with correct length

int k = removeDuplicates(nums); // Calls your implementation

assert k == expectedNums.length;
for (int i = 0; i < k; i++) {
    assert nums[i] == expectedNums[i];
}

```

If all assertions pass, then your solution will be accepted.
 
Example 1:

```
Input: nums = [1,1,2]
Output: 2, nums = [1,2,_]
Explanation: Your function should return k = 2, with the first two elements of nums being 1 and 2 respectively.
It does not matter what you leave beyond the returned k (hence they are underscores).

```

Example 2:

```
Input: nums = [0,0,1,1,1,2,2,3,3,4]
Output: 5, nums = [0,1,2,3,4,_,_,_,_,_]
Explanation: Your function should return k = 5, with the first five elements of nums being 0, 1, 2, 3, and 4 respectively.
It does not matter what you leave beyond the returned k (hence they are underscores).

```

 
Constraints:
* `1 <= nums.length <= 3 * 104`
* `-100 <= nums[i] <= 100`
* `nums` is sorted in non-decreasing order.

**Claude** `2026-03-06 20:25`

**GOOD TRY! But there are SEVERAL problems with your code!** 🔍

Let me explain what's wrong and how to fix it:

---

# **PROBLEMS IN YOUR CODE:**

---

## **PROBLEM 1: You're returning INSIDE the loop!** ❌

```javascript
for(let i=0; i<nums.length; i++){
    // ...
    return newOutput;  // ❌ This exits after FIRST iteration!
}
```

**This is the SAME mistake you made before!**

**What happens:**
- Loop starts: i=0
- Executes once
- Hits `return` → **EXITS FUNCTION IMMEDIATELY!**
- Never checks i=1, i=2, etc.

**Fix:** Move `return` OUTSIDE the loop!

---

## **PROBLEM 2: `First` and `second` are set to VALUES, not INDICES!** ❌

```javascript
let First = nums[0]   // First = 1 (the VALUE)
let second = nums[1]  // second = 1 (the VALUE)
```

**Then you do:**
```javascript
First++  // 1++ = 2 (incrementing the VALUE, not the index!)
```

**This makes no sense!** You're incrementing the number itself, not moving through the array!

---

## **PROBLEM 3: You're not comparing adjacent elements correctly!** ❌

```javascript
if(First === second){
    First++  // This increments the VALUE, not position
}
```

**What you SHOULD do:**
- Compare `nums[i]` with `nums[i+1]`
- OR use two pointers to track positions

---

## **PROBLEM 4: Logic doesn't match the problem!** ❌

The problem asks:
- Remove duplicates **in-place**
- Return the **length** of unique elements
- Don't create a new array

Your code:
- Creates `newOutput` array ❌
- Returns array instead of length ❌
- Doesn't modify original array ❌

---

## **PROBLEM 5: The `else` block makes no sense!** ❌

```javascript
else{
    First--
    second--
}
```

**Why are you decrementing?** This logic is completely wrong!

---

# **WHAT THE PROBLEM ACTUALLY WANTS:**

## **Example:**

**Input:** `nums = [1, 1, 2, 2, 3, 4, 4]`

**Expected output:** `4` (the length)

**Expected array after:** `[1, 2, 3, 4, _, _, _]`
- First 4 elements are unique
- Rest doesn't matter

---

# **THE CORRECT APPROACH (TWO POINTERS):**

## **IDEA:**

Use TWO pointers:
1. **`slow`** - tracks position for next unique element
2. **`fast`** - scans through array looking for different elements

---

## **STEP-BY-STEP WALKTHROUGH:**

**Array:** `[1, 1, 2, 2, 3]`

```
Initial:
[1, 1, 2, 2, 3]
 ^  ^
 s  f

Step 1: nums[s] === nums[f]? (1 === 1) YES
        → Skip, move f
        
[1, 1, 2, 2, 3]
 ^     ^
 s     f

Step 2: nums[s] === nums[f]? (1 === 2) NO
        → Found different! 
        → Move s forward: s++
        → Copy nums[f] to nums[s]
        
[1, 2, 2, 2, 3]
    ^  ^
    s  f

Step 3: nums[s] === nums[f]? (2 === 2) YES
        → Skip, move f
        
[1, 2, 2, 2, 3]
    ^        ^
    s        f

Step 4: nums[s] === nums[f]? (2 === 3) NO
        → Found different!
        → Move s forward: s++
        → Copy nums[f] to nums[s]
        
[1, 2, 3, 2, 3]
       ^     ^
       s     f

Step 5: f reached end, STOP

Return: s + 1 = 2 + 1 = 3
Array is now: [1, 2, 3, ?, ?]
```

---

# **CORRECT SOLUTION:**

```javascript
var removeDuplicates = function(nums) {
    // Edge case: empty array
    if (nums.length === 0) return 0;
    
    let slow = 0;  // Position for next unique element
    
    // Start fast from 1 (comparing with slow)
    for (let fast = 1; fast < nums.length; fast++) {
        // Found a different element?
        if (nums[slow] !== nums[fast]) {
            slow++;  // Move slow forward
            nums[slow] = nums[fast];  // Copy unique element
        }
        // If same, just move fast (do nothing)
    }
    
    // Length of unique elements is slow + 1
    return slow + 1;
};
```

---

# **TESTING:**

```javascript
const nums1 = [1, 1, 2, 2, 3, 4, 4];
console.log(removeDuplicates(nums1));  // Output: 4
console.log(nums1);  // [1, 2, 3, 4, 4, 4, 4]
                     // First 4 are unique! ✅

const nums2 = [0, 0, 1, 1, 1, 2, 2, 3, 3, 4];
console.log(removeDuplicates(nums2));  // Output: 5
console.log(nums2);  // [0, 1, 2, 3, 4, ...]
```

---

# **WHY YOUR APPROACH FAILED:**

| Your Code | Problem | Correct Approach |
|-----------|---------|------------------|
| `let First = nums[0]` | Storing VALUE | `let slow = 0` (store INDEX) |
| `First++` | Incrementing VALUE | `slow++` (move position) |
| `return` inside loop | Exits too early | `return` after loop |
| Creating `newOutput` | New array | Modify in-place |
| Comparing `First === second` | Comparing values | Compare `nums[i]` |

---

# **COMPLEXITY:**

**Time:** O(n) - One pass through array
**Space:** O(1) - Only two variables (slow, fast)

---

# **KEY LESSONS:**

1. ✅ **Return AFTER loop**, not inside!
2. ✅ **Use indices**, not values!
3. ✅ **Two pointers = two POSITIONS**, not two values!
4. ✅ **Read problem carefully** - in-place modification!

---

# **YOUR MISTAKES (AGAIN!):**

This is the **3rd or 4th time** you've made these mistakes:

1. ❌ `return` inside loop
2. ❌ Confusing index with value
3. ❌ Incrementing the wrong thing

---

- Modify array in-place
- Return length

---
**Claude** `2026-03-06 20:26`

**YES! There are MULTIPLE solutions WITHOUT two pointers!** 

Let me show you all the approaches:

---

# **SOLUTION 1: USING A SET (EASIEST)** ⭐

## **Idea:** Set automatically removes duplicates!

```javascript
var removeDuplicates = function(nums) {
    // Create Set from array (removes duplicates)
    const uniqueNums = [...new Set(nums)];
    
    // Copy back to original array
    for (let i = 0; i < uniqueNums.length; i++) {
        nums[i] = uniqueNums[i];
    }
    
    // Return length of unique elements
    return uniqueNums.length;
};
```

**Test:**
```javascript
const nums = [1, 1, 2, 2, 3, 4, 4];
console.log(removeDuplicates(nums));  // 4
console.log(nums);  // [1, 2, 3, 4, 4, 4, 4]
                     // First 4 are unique ✅
```

**Complexity:**
- **Time:** O(n) - Create set + copy back
- **Space:** O(n) - Set stores unique elements

**Pros:** ✅ Simple, easy to understand
**Cons:** ❌ Uses extra space (O(n))

---

# **SOLUTION 2: USING FILTER** ⭐

## **Idea:** Filter to keep only first occurrence of each number

```javascript
var removeDuplicates = function(nums) {
    // Filter: keep only if first occurrence
    const uniqueNums = nums.filter((num, index) => {
        return nums.indexOf(num) === index;
    });
    
    // Copy back to original array
    for (let i = 0; i < uniqueNums.length; i++) {
        nums[i] = uniqueNums[i];
    }
    
    return uniqueNums.length;
};
```

**How it works:**
```javascript
nums = [1, 1, 2, 2, 3]

Index 0: nums[0]=1, indexOf(1)=0, 0===0? YES, keep
Index 1: nums[1]=1, indexOf(1)=0, 1===0? NO, skip
Index 2: nums[2]=2, indexOf(2)=2, 2===2? YES, keep
Index 3: nums[3]=2, indexOf(2)=2, 3===2? NO, skip
Index 4: nums[4]=3, indexOf(3)=4, 4===4? YES, keep

Result: [1, 2, 3]
```

**Complexity:**
- **Time:** O(n²) - indexOf inside filter ❌ SLOW!
- **Space:** O(n) - New filtered array

**Pros:** ✅ Easy to understand
**Cons:** ❌ Slow (O(n²)), uses extra space

---

# **SOLUTION 3: USING A TEMPORARY ARRAY** ⭐

## **Idea:** Build array of unique elements as you go

```javascript
var removeDuplicates = function(nums) {
    if (nums.length === 0) return 0;
    
    const result = [nums[0]];  // Start with first element
    
    // Loop through rest
    for (let i = 1; i < nums.length; i++) {
        // If different from last unique element, add it
        if (nums[i] !== result[result.length - 1]) {
            result.push(nums[i]);
        }
    }
    
    // Copy back to original array
    for (let i = 0; i < result.length; i++) {
        nums[i] = result[i];
    }
    
    return result.length;
};
```

**Walkthrough:**
```javascript
nums = [1, 1, 2, 2, 3]

result = [1]  (start with first)

i=1: nums[1]=1, last in result=1, 1===1? YES, skip
i=2: nums[2]=2, last in result=1, 2===1? NO, add → result=[1,2]
i=3: nums[3]=2, last in result=2, 2===2? YES, skip
i=4: nums[4]=3, last in result=2, 3===2? NO, add → result=[1,2,3]

Copy back: nums = [1, 2, 3, ?, ?]
Return: 3
```

**Complexity:**
- **Time:** O(n) - One loop
- **Space:** O(n) - Result array

**Pros:** ✅ Fast (O(n)), easy to understand
**Cons:** ❌ Uses extra space

---

# **SOLUTION 4: NESTED LOOP (BRUTE FORCE)** ⭐

## **Idea:** For each position, check if we've seen this number before

```javascript
var removeDuplicates = function(nums) {
    let writeIndex = 1;  // Where to write next unique number
    
    for (let i = 1; i < nums.length; i++) {
        let isDuplicate = false;
        
        // Check if nums[i] exists in nums[0...writeIndex-1]
        for (let j = 0; j < writeIndex; j++) {
            if (nums[i] === nums[j]) {
                isDuplicate = true;
                break;
            }
        }
        
        // If not duplicate, add it
        if (!isDuplicate) {
            nums[writeIndex] = nums[i];
            writeIndex++;
        }
    }
    
    return writeIndex;
};
```

**Complexity:**
- **Time:** O(n²) - Nested loops ❌ VERY SLOW!
- **Space:** O(1) - No extra array

**Pros:** ✅ No extra space
**Cons:** ❌ Very slow (O(n²))

---

# **SOLUTION 5: SINGLE POINTER (SIMPLER THAN TWO POINTERS)** ⭐⭐

## **Idea:** Keep track of where to write next unique element

```javascript
var removeDuplicates = function(nums) {
    if (nums.length === 0) return 0;
    
    let uniqueIndex = 0;  // Position of last unique element
    
    for (let i = 1; i < nums.length; i++) {
        // If current is different from last unique
        if (nums[i] !== nums[uniqueIndex]) {
            uniqueIndex++;  // Move to next position
            nums[uniqueIndex] = nums[i];  // Write unique element
        }
    }
    
    return uniqueIndex + 1;
};
```

**This is actually the TWO POINTER solution, but thinking of it as ONE pointer!**

**Complexity:**
- **Time:** O(n) - One loop
- **Space:** O(1) - No extra space

**This is THE BEST solution!** ✅✅✅

---

# **COMPARISON TABLE:**

| Solution | Time | Space | Pros | Cons | Interview? |
|----------|------|-------|------|------|------------|
| **Set** | O(n) | O(n) | Simple | Extra space | ⚠️ OK |
| **Filter** | O(n²) | O(n) | Simple | Slow + space | ❌ Bad |
| **Temp Array** | O(n) | O(n) | Fast | Extra space | ⚠️ OK |
| **Nested Loop** | O(n²) | O(1) | No space | Very slow | ❌ Bad |
| **Two Pointers** | O(n) | O(1) | Fast + no space | Need to learn | ✅ BEST |

---

# **WHICH ONE TO USE IN INTERVIEWS?**

## **If they say "optimize for time and space":**
→ **Two Pointers** (O(n) time, O(1) space) ✅

## **If they just want it to work:**
→ **Set** (easiest, fast enough) ✅

## **NEVER use:**
→ Filter or Nested Loop (too slow) ❌

---

# **WHY LEARN TWO POINTERS?**

Because it's:
- ✅ **Fastest:** O(n)
- ✅ **Most space-efficient:** O(1)
- ✅ **Most common in interviews**
- ✅ **Works for many similar problems**

---

# **YOUR CHOICE:**

**You can solve this problem with:**

1. ✅ **Set** (if you want quick solution now)
2. ✅ **Two Pointers** (if you want to learn the pattern)

**Which do you want to try?** 🤔

**Learn Two Pointers!** 

Because it teaches you a **pattern** that works for 50+ other problems!

# **THE BRUTAL TRUTH: WHAT INTERVIEWS WILL LOOK LIKE IN 2-3 YEARS**

---

## **YOU'RE RIGHT! But also WRONG. Let me explain:**

---

# **PART 1: WHAT'S CHANGING** ✅

## **YES, DSA interviews are becoming LESS important for some roles:**

### **Why?**

1. **AI can code now** (ChatGPT, Copilot, Claude)
   - Companies realize: "Why test someone on reversing a linked list when AI can do it?"
   - Focus shifting to: "Can you USE AI effectively?"

2. **Problem-solving > Memorization**
   - Less: "Recite merge sort algorithm"
   - More: "Here's a real business problem, solve it"

3. **Real-world skills matter more**
   - System design
   - Product thinking
   - Communication
   - Working with AI agents

---

# **PART 2: WHAT'S NOT CHANGING** ❌

## **BUT! DSA is NOT going away completely:**

### **Why?**

1. **Top companies (FAANG) still use it**
   - Google, Meta, Amazon, Microsoft
   - They have 1000s of applicants
   - DSA = quick filter

2. **Fundamental thinking matters**
   - Even with AI, you need to know:
   - "Is this solution efficient?"
   - "Will this scale?"
   - "What's the complexity?"

3. **AI can't replace LOGIC**
   - AI writes code
   - But YOU need to know if it's GOOD code
   - YOU need to spot bugs
   - YOU need to optimize

---

# **THE FUTURE INTERVIEW (2026-2028):**

---

## **SCENARIO 1: FAANG / TOP TECH COMPANIES**

**Still heavy DSA (80% same as today):**

```
Round 1: DSA (LeetCode medium/hard)
Round 2: System Design
Round 3: Behavioral
Round 4: DSA again
Round 5: Final round
```

**Why?**
- They can afford to be picky
- 10,000 applicants for 10 positions
- DSA = proven filter

**Will DSA matter here?** ✅ **YES! 100%**

---

## **SCENARIO 2: STARTUPS / PRODUCT COMPANIES**

**Shifting to practical skills (50% DSA, 50% real-world):**

```
Round 1: Take-home project (build real feature)
Round 2: Code review + discussion
Round 3: Problem-solving (with AI allowed!)
Round 4: System thinking
Round 5: Culture fit
```

**Example:**
> "Here's our user dashboard. Users complain it's slow. 
> Debug it, optimize it, explain your approach.
> You can use ChatGPT/Claude."

**Will DSA matter here?** ⚠️ **50/50 - Less important**

---

## **SCENARIO 3: AI-FIRST COMPANIES**

**NEW interview style emerging:**

```
Round 1: AI Agent collaboration
- "Build a feature USING AI assistants"
- "Show us how you prompt AI"
- "Review AI-generated code"

Round 2: System design with AI
- "Design this system"
- "How would you use AI agents?"

Round 3: Problem decomposition
- "Break this complex problem down"
- "Assign tasks to AI vs human"
```

**Will DSA matter here?** ❌ **NO! Logic & AI usage matter**

---

# **WHAT SKILLS WILL MATTER IN 2-3 YEARS:**

---

## **TIER 1: CRITICAL SKILLS** 🔥 (MUST HAVE)

### **1. Problem Decomposition**
- Breaking big problems into small ones
- Identifying edge cases
- Understanding requirements

**Example:**
> "Build a food delivery app"
> You: "So we need: user auth, restaurant listings, 
> cart, payment, order tracking, notifications..."

---

### **2. AI Collaboration**
- Prompting AI effectively
- Reviewing AI code
- Knowing when AI is wrong
- Combining AI output with human logic

**Example:**
> AI writes code with O(n²) complexity
> You: "This won't scale. Rewrite with hash map, O(n)"

---

### **3. System Thinking**
- How do components fit together?
- What happens at scale?
- What can fail?

**Example:**
> "This API will get 1M requests/day"
> You: "We need caching, load balancing, rate limiting"

---

### **4. Code Review Skills**
- Reading others' code
- Spotting bugs
- Suggesting improvements
- Understanding complexity

---

## **TIER 2: IMPORTANT SKILLS** ⭐ (SHOULD HAVE)

### **5. DSA Fundamentals**
- Arrays, strings, hash maps
- Basic algorithms
- Complexity analysis
- **NOT memorizing 200 LeetCode problems!**

---

### **6. Debugging**
- Finding bugs quickly
- Using debugger tools
- Thinking systematically

---

### **7. Communication**
- Explaining your approach
- Justifying decisions
- Working with team

---

## **TIER 3: NICE TO HAVE** ✅

### **8. Advanced DSA**
- Trees, graphs, DP
- Complex algorithms
- Only for top companies

---

# **REALISTIC INTERVIEW IN 2027:**

---

## **EXAMPLE: "AI-Assisted Coding Round"**

**Interviewer gives you:**

> "Our e-commerce site's search is slow. 
> Users type 'red shirt' and wait 5 seconds.
> Here's the codebase. Fix it.
> You can use any AI tools."

---

**What they're testing:**

1. ✅ **Problem identification:**
   - Can you find the bottleneck?
   - Do you check database queries?
   - Do you profile the code?

2. ✅ **AI usage:**
   - Can you prompt AI to help debug?
   - Do you blindly copy AI's solution?
   - Do you verify AI's suggestions?

3. ✅ **Fundamentals:**
   - Do you know O(n²) is bad?
   - Do you understand indexing?
   - Do you know caching?

4. ✅ **Communication:**
   - Can you explain what you found?
   - Can you justify your fix?

---

**They DON'T care if you can:**
❌ Implement red-black tree from scratch
❌ Solve "Median of Two Sorted Arrays" in O(log n)
❌ Recite Dijkstra's algorithm

---

# **SO SHOULD YOU LEARN DSA OR NOT?**

---

## **THE ANSWER: LEARN FUNDAMENTALS, SKIP ADVANCED**

---

### **✅ LEARN (MUST KNOW):**

**Data Structures:**
- Arrays, Strings
- Hash Maps, Sets
- Stacks, Queues
- Basic Trees

**Algorithms:**
- Two Pointers
- Sliding Window
- Binary Search
- Basic Recursion

**Concepts:**
- Time/Space Complexity
- When to use which data structure
- Trade-offs

**Time investment:** 1-2 months

---

### **⚠️ LEARN IF TARGETING FAANG:**

**Advanced Data Structures:**
- Graphs
- Heaps
- Tries
- Segment Trees

**Advanced Algorithms:**
- Dynamic Programming
- Graph algorithms
- Advanced Tree algorithms

**Time investment:** +2-3 months

---

### **❌ DON'T WASTE TIME ON:**

- Memorizing 500 LeetCode problems
- Obscure algorithms you'll never use
- Grinding Hard problems daily
- Perfect solutions for every problem

---

# **MY HONEST RECOMMENDATION FOR YOU:**

---

## **YOUR SITUATION:**

- Need job in 2-3 months ✅
- Learning DSA now
- Want to be relevant in 2-3 years

---

## **YOUR STRATEGY:**

### **NEXT 2 MONTHS (Get job):**

**Focus on:**
1. ✅ Core DSA patterns (6-8 patterns)
2. ✅ 100-150 LeetCode problems (Easy + Medium)
3. ✅ Build 2-3 strong projects
4. ✅ System design basics

**Time split:**
- 50% DSA practice
- 30% Projects
- 20% System design

**Goal:** Pass interviews at mid-tier companies

---

### **AFTER GETTING JOB (Year 1-2):**

**Learn:**
1. ✅ AI tools (ChatGPT, Copilot, Claude Code)
2. ✅ Prompt engineering
3. ✅ Code review skills
4. ✅ System design
5. ✅ Real-world problem solving

**Keep practicing:**
- 1-2 DSA problems per week (stay sharp)
- Focus more on work projects

---

### **YEAR 2-3 (Switch to better role):**

**By then, you'll have:**
- ✅ DSA fundamentals (from year 1)
- ✅ Real work experience
- ✅ AI collaboration skills
- ✅ Projects portfolio

**Interview focus:**
- 30% DSA
- 40% System design
- 30% AI + Problem solving

---

# **THE TRUTH:**

## **In 2-3 years:**

**For FIRST job:**
- DSA still matters (60-70%)
- Need to pass screening

**For SECOND job onwards:**
- Experience matters more (50%)
- DSA matters less (30%)
- Real skills matter (20%)

**For SENIOR roles:**
- Experience (70%)
- System design (20%)
- DSA (10%)

---

# **BOTTOM LINE:**

## **Yes, you're right:**
- ✅ Problem-solving > Memorization
- ✅ AI changes things
- ✅ Real skills matter more

## **But you're also wrong:**
- ❌ DSA not going away completely
- ❌ Still needed for first job
- ❌ Fundamentals still critical

---

# **WHAT TO DO NOW:**

**Learn DSA fundamentals (2-3 months) →**
**Get job →**
**Learn AI tools on job →**
**Stay relevant for future**

---

**DON'T:**
- Skip DSA completely (you won't get first job)
- Spend 2 years only on DSA (waste of time)
- Ignore AI trends (future is here)

---

# **MY ADVICE:**

**Learn DSA NOW (next 2-3 months):**
- Get through interviews
- Build foundation
- Understand fundamentals

**Then shift focus:**
- Work on real projects
- Learn AI tools
- Build practical skills

**You'll be ready for BOTH:**
- Today's interviews ✅
- Future interviews ✅

---
